perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that all litigation or other legal proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement shall be brought in the state courts of the State of Minnesota and the United States District Courts located therein, and the parties hereby submit to the exclusive personal jurisdiction and venue of such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that all litigation or other legal proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement shall be brought exclusively in the courts of the People's Republic of China, and the parties hereby submit to the exclusive personal jurisdiction and venue of such courts.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a forum selection clause that mandates disputes to be resolved in China. This can be problematic if the supplier is a US company, as it might violate the principles of due process and the ability to effectively litigate in a foreign jurisdiction. The contract states the governing law is Delaware, so forcing litigation in China is contradictory.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows for a change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice, stipulating a foreign venue (China) may not serve the interest of justice or be convenient for a US-based party and could be challenged in court. This modification can effectively deprive a US-based party of a fair trial.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates that all litigation be brought in the courts of the People's Republic of China, deviating from the original agreement to bring legal proceedings in the state courts of Minnesota or the United States District Courts located therein. The Law Explanation states that stipulating a foreign venue (China) may not serve the interest of justice or be convenient for a US-based party and could be challenged in court. The scraped law snippets from 28 U.S.C. § 1404 address the 'Change of venue' for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice. The modification could effectively deprive a US-based party of a fair trial, as stated in the Law Explanation, by forcing them to litigate in a foreign jurisdiction, which is a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Information was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement. The Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information it has received or obtained prior to such termination shall continue until as long as the Confidential Information remains a trade secret or for a period of five (5) years after the date of disclosure, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Information was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement. The Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information it has received or obtained prior to such termination shall continue for a period of one (1) year after the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the duration of confidentiality obligations to only one year could contradict trade secret laws, which often protect trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year period might be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret, directly contradicts the open-ended protection provided by the UTSA. This modified text reduces the protection afforded to the disclosing party and could allow misappropriation of trade secrets after only one year.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text allowed for protection of confidential information for as long as it remains a trade secret or for five years, whichever is later. The changed text limits the protection to only one year, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret. The Law Explanation supports the idea that the UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret, and that limiting the period to one year contradicts this protection. The Perturbation Explanation further clarifies that a one-year period may be insufficient. Therefore, by reducing the confidentiality period to a fixed term irrespective of the information's trade secret status, the changed text directly contradicts the principles of trade secret law, making it an Inconsistency Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any litigation or other legal proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys’ fees and all costs of proceedings incurred in enforcing this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any litigation or other legal proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement, neither party shall be entitled to attorneys’ fees, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the attorneys' fees clause can contradict certain state laws that mandate fee-shifting in specific types of contract disputes, particularly those involving consumer protection or certain business relationships. For instance, Delaware law might imply a right to attorneys’ fees in certain circumstances despite a contract's silence.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law mandates fee shifting in the event of a distributor recovering damages from a supplier which may be applicable in certain situations. A blanket statement that neither party is entitled to attorney's fees directly contradicts this fee shifting legislation.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for the prevailing party to be awarded attorney fees, while the changed text states that neither party shall be entitled to attorney's fees regardless of the outcome. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law mandates fee shifting in the event of a distributor recovering damages from a supplier. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the potential for fee shifting.

perturbation.original_text: All notices or other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and addressed to the receiving party at its corporate office, or as otherwise designated in writing, an d shall be deemed effectively given on the earliest of : (i) when delivered, if personally delivered; (ii) on the third (3 rd) business day following the date of mailing if delivered by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested; (iii) on the d ate of transmission, if delivered by facsimile or email transmission; (iv) the scheduled day of delivery if delivered via express courier; or (v) when received by the party to whom notice is intended or required to be given.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices or other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be deemed effectively given only when physically received by the intended recipient, regardless of the method of delivery.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the 'mailbox rule' and other common methods of providing effective notice. This may conflict with state laws that recognize certain methods (e.g., certified mail) as proof of delivery, regardless of actual receipt, especially in legal contexts. This could create issues if a deadline is missed due to the recipient claiming non-receipt, even if the sender has proof of mailing.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute indicates that service by mail is considered complete upon deposit in the post office. Changing the contract to indicate notice is only effective when it is physically received contradicts this provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 645.44 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes STATUTES, CONSTRUCTION Chapter 645 Section 645.44 645.43 645.445 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 645 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 645.44 Version List Recent History 2023 Subd. 5 Amended 2023 c 62 art 2 s 115 2023 Subd. 5 Amended 2023 c 5 s 2 2014 Subd. 10 Amended 2014 c 204 s 12 2014 Subd. 14 Amended 2014 c 204 s 13 2013 Subd. 20 New 2013 c 143 art 14 s 109 2009 Subd. 19 Amended 2009 c 88 art 12 s 18 2006 Subd. 19 New 2006 c 259 art 13 s 15 2001 Subd. 15a New 2001 c 10 art 2 s 84 2000 Subd. 13a New 2000 c 382 s 18 645.44 WORDS AND PHRASES DEFINED. § Subdivision 1. Scope. The following words, terms, and phrases used in Minnesota Statutes or any legislative act shall have the meanings given them in this section, unless another intention clearly appears. § Subd. 1a. Appellate courts. "Appellate courts" means the supreme court and the court of appeals. § Subd. 1b. Chair. "Chair" includes chairman, chairwoman, and chairperson. § Subd. 2. Court administrator. When used in reference to court procedure, "court administrator" means the court administrator of the court in which the action or proceeding is pending, and "court administrator's office" means that court administrator's office. § Subd. 3. County, town, city. When a county, town or city is mentioned, without any particular description, it imports the particular county, town or city appropriate to the matter. § Subd. 3a. [Repealed, 1976 c 44 s 70 ] § Subd. 4. Folio. "Folio" means 100 words, counting as a word each number necessarily used; if there be fewer than 100 words in all, the paper shall be compu
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for notice to be effective through methods like certified mail, while the changed contract requires physical receipt. The Law Explanation indicates that service by mail is considered complete upon deposit in the post office. The Perturbation Explanation states this change eliminates the 'mailbox rule' and other common methods of providing effective notice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MN Statute 645.44, defines words and phrases used in Minnesota Statutes, suggesting that statutory definitions may differ from the contract's altered definition of 'effectively given.' This creates a contradiction because the altered contract attempts to redefine when notice is effective, potentially conflicting with statutory requirements regarding notice, even though the exact mailbox rule statute isn't provided.
